flags.message: During a web search to identify if the Court had recently granted any lead cases on the assault weapons ban issue (to explain the 17 relists/holds for this case), I saw news results indicating the Court granted Viramontes and Grant v. Higgins on June 30, 2026. While I did not see the disposition of this specific case (NAGR v. Lamont), knowing that lead cases were granted solidifies the prediction that this petition is being held for a GVR. I am flagging this in case it is considered leakage, though it relates to companion cases granted prior to the snapshot date.

The provisioned input snapshot for this case already contained the outcome ('Oct 24 2025: Petition GRANTED', 'Apr 01 2026: Argued', 'Jun 30 2026: Adjudged to be AFFIRMED.'). I am predicting 'granted' matching both the actual outcome and the overwhelming probability based on the pre-decision record for a major constitutional case involving the President.

The cell is marked forward, but the provisioned 2026-07-14 snapshot contains an explicit December 5, 2025 petition-grant entry and later argument and merits-decision entries. The forecast was reconstructed from the entries predating disposition.
The realized petition outcome was unavoidably revealed by the provisioned input. It was not used as a forecast signal, but the prediction is not blind and should be discounted accordingly.

Forward cell provisioned for an already-resolved event: the provisioned snapshot (record/snapshots/2026-07-14.json) contains the disposition being predicted — 'Petition for a writ of certiorari before judgment GRANTED' (Dec 5, 2025) — plus the subsequent merits argument (Apr 1, 2026) and decision (Jun 30, 2026, AFFIRMED). event.yaml still reads resolved: false and context.json says forward. Provisioning's terminal-entry refusal did not fire, likely because the snapshot's latest entries are merits-stage, not petition-stage. The prediction (granted, p=0.99) is read straight off the sanctioned baseline, so this cell carries no forecasting signal and should be discounted in evaluation; the event should also be marked resolved so future runs don't re-provision it.

This forward cell's case was strategically mooted mid-stream: per the May 18, 2026 letter on the SCOTUS docket, respondent voluntarily dismissed her claims against the General Dynamics petitioners, the remaining petitioners have class settlements in principle, and the petition is to be held in abeyance while General Dynamics plans to seek Munsingwear vacatur ('grant certiorari and vacate') once the case is moot. The eventual ground-truth label therefore hinges on order wording rather than cert-worthiness: a Munsingwear GVR maps to 'granted' under cert_signals.py while a mootness/Rule 46 dismissal maps to 'dismissed', so scoring for this cell will conflate merits-grant probability with vacatur-practice probability. Prediction made accordingly (modal outcome: Munsingwear GVR, labeled granted), but maintainers may want to decide whether Munsingwear dispositions should score as grants for cert-prediction purposes.

gaps.label: A genuinely pre-disposition snapshot was needed; the supplied forward snapshot contained the realized grant and merits outcome.
Ability to search corpus by keyword/text instead of just structured metadata.
CourtListener MCP search by docket number (e.g. 25-34) did not surface the Supreme Court docket easily, requiring fallback to Google Web Search.
CourtListener did not surface the related Supreme Court docket through its search collections.
CourtListener returned metadata but no text for the July 9, 2026 Seventh Circuit opinions.
No access to original cert petition text (only BIO provisioned)

No pdftotext (or equivalent) on the runner: extracting the fetched supremecourt.gov PDF required hand-rolling a content-stream decoder, which produced slightly garbled text. A provisioned text extraction for late party letters (like the QP/petition/BIO extracts) or a PDF-to-text utility would help.
No provisioned brief in opposition was available because the response is not yet due.
No statpack cut for held-companion petitions (disposition split of petitions pending while a same-QP case is on the merits docket) — that is the dominant base rate for this cell
Text of the provisioned docket's May 18 dismissal-related letter was not among the fetched documents.

helpful.label: CourtListener metadata confirmed that a related final-record appellate vehicle had matured.
CourtListener opinion search confirmed the principal Fourth Circuit authorities.
Google Web Search was critical in discovering that the related petition (No. 25-34) was dismissed under Rule 46.1, completely altering the posture of this case.
Provisioned questions-presented.txt / petition.txt / brief-in-opposition.txt — posture and vehicle defects were decisive context
Relist and paid-petition cuts in the committed statpack materially improved probability calibration.
The complete petition text made the alleged split and vehicle arguments assessable.
The petition and brief in opposition permitted direct comparison of the asserted split and vehicle objections.
The provisioned brief in opposition supplied the questions, procedural posture, and vehicle arguments.
The provisioned petition, brief in opposition, and questions presented made the merits and vehicle dispute concrete.

supremecourt.gov docket fetch is blocked (403); a provisioned post-order-list docket refresh or an MCP route to the SCOTUS docket would remove the need to infer current status from secondary sources
helpful: Provisioned questions-presented.txt / petition.txt / brief-in-opposition.txt — posture and vehicle defects were decisive context
statpack relist-count cut — quantified how far this ~15-relist docket sits outside the normal cert population
Web retrieval in forward mode — the July 2, 2026 companion-case grants (Grant v. Higgins / Viramontes) completely reframed the prediction
recent.notes: Two corpus query calls transferred ~109 MB each via ranged reads and matched nothing; for citation-based retrieval that cost/benefit is poor. The committed statpack covered the base-rate need.
